research_answer: Interestingly, when researchers control for open forest (South Africa's position in the product space), there is no statistically significant tendency for South Africa to diversify faster or slower than would be predicted by its structural position alone. This is a meaningful finding: for some countries — like Spain, Romania, El Salvador, and Zimbabwe — there are important factors beyond product space location that accelerate or retard structural transformation. But for South Africa, its predicament is largely explained by where it sits in the product space itself. The country's peripheral position, with few densely connected nearby products, constrains the pace of diversification. Fix the product space problem — move into better-connected products — and the structural transformation challenge largely resolves itself.
supporting_quotes: So while some factors other than location in the product space are particularly important in the cases of Spain, Romania, El Salvador, and Zimbabwe, in the case of South Africa there is no statistically significant tendency towards rapid or slow structural transformation controlling for open forest. That is, in the case of South Africa, much of its structural transformation predicament boils down to its placement in the product space.
